Be careful with its use, its only optimized for the languge itself TableScript Structs TabIndex Represents an Index for a Table Interfaces IImportResolver Enums TabIndexMode TabScriptErrorType

docs/example.html.title: Example program | TableScript
docs/example.html.summary: Example program Here is a program showing virtually all features: // IMPORTANT // In this file, next to print() calls, the supposed output will be shown in a comment along with a commentary if needed //Importing the standard library for print and other functions import stdlib; //Table variables tab global = ["h", "e", "l", "l", "o"]; //Concatenation operator +: table concatenation tab greeting = global + [" ", "world"]; print(greeting); //hello world //+= concatenation operator: concatenates itself and sets greeting += " stranger"; print(greeting); //hello world stranger //String literals are handled like length 1 tables: tab eq = "abcd" == ["abcd"]; //Dollar strings with full expressions (syntax highlighter migh break because of this) tab doll = $"The message of the day is '{greeting + "!"}'"; print(doll); //The message of the day is 'hello world stranger!' //True and false handled with length: <0 or 0 is false, >0 is true tab true = ["anything"]; tab false = []; //If statements if true{ print("true"); //true } if false{ print("false"); } //Ternary operator ?: print(eq ? "equal" : "not equal"); //equal //negation operator !: returns [] (false) when the table has length greater than zero and [""] (true) when length is 0. This is the general concept of truthiness tab t = !["h"]; //difference operator -: removes elements tab h = ["a", "a", "b"] - ["a"]; print(h); //ab //-= difference operator also available h -= "a"; print(h); //b //product operator *: cartesian product tab p = ["a", "b"] * ["c", "d"]; print(p); //acbcadbd p = ["a", "b"] * 3; print(p); //ababab p = ["a", "b"]; p *= 3; //*= also available print(p); //ababab p = 3 * ["a", "b"]; print(p); //aaabbb //Equality operators == and != print(["a", "b"] != ["ab"] ? "true" : "false"); //true print(["a", "b"] == ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //true print(["a", "b"] == "ab" ? "true" : "false"); //false //Reduction operator ^: joins all elements into a single string print(["a", "b", "c"]^ == "abc" ? "true" : "false"); //true //Decomposition operator %: separates all charachters of all elements into their own elements print(["a", "b", "c"] == "abc"% ? "true" : "false"); //true //containment operator @: checks if all elements are contained print(["a", "b"] @ ["c", "d", "a", "n", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //true print(["a", "b", "m"] @ ["c", "d", "a", "n", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //false //Comparison operators <, <=, >, >=: they compare lengths of tables print(["1"] > ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //false print(4 > ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //true //Numbers are tables as well: 2 is the same as ["", ""], with empty strings and length 2. print(3); //Nothing will be seen (empty strings) print(3 == ["", "", ""] ? "true" : "false"); //true //length: retuns a number table of the length: ["a", "b"].length => ["", ""] (2); print(2 == ["a", "b"].length ? "true" : "false"); //true //Element access tab abc = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f"]; print(abc.0); //a print(abc.1); //b //Negative index: last elements print(abc.-1); //f print(abc.-2); //e print(abc.random); //Random element //Expression index access with []: full expressions print(abc[0 + 1]); //b tab oob = abc.100; //Obviously out of bounds, returns [] print(oob); //Nothing will be seen (empty table) print(abc.-100); //Nothing will be seen (empty table) (Obviously out of bounds, returns []) //Element setting tab nums = ["0", "2", "3"]; nums.0 = "1"; print(nums); //123 nums[1] = "a"; print(nums); //1a3 //Expression index setting with []: full expressions nums[1 + 2] = "g"; print(nums); //1a3g //Range access: [index, length]. Handles negative indexes, negative lengths and expressions print(abc[0, 3]); //abc print(abc[1, 3]); //bcd print(abc[1, 300]); //Out of bounds, auto crops: bcdef print(abc[-1, 1]); //f print(abc[-1, -1]); //e print(abc[random, 2]); //random consecutive elements print(abc[1 + ["n", "t"], 1]); //Expression: d //Grouping with () tab g = (abc + "k")%.length > (16 - (4 + 2)); print(g ? "true" : "false"); //false //Literal building: flattening of other tables tab all = [abc, "+", nums, ["j", ["k", "l", abc], "k"]]; print(all); //abcdef+1a3gjklabcdefk //While statements with else clauses(if no break was invoked) tab i = 0; while i < 5{ print("elem"); i += 1; }else{ print("finish"); } //Do statements with optionally else clause i = 0; do{ print("do"); i += 1; }while i < 6; //Visual separator print("next loop"); //next loop i = 0; do{ print("do"); i += 1; }while i < 6 else{ print("finish do"); } //Foreach foreach letter @ abc + nums{ print(letter); } //Lexical scoping tab k = "out"; if 1{ print(k); //out tab k = "in"; print(k); //in } print(k); //out //Function call print(plusA("h")); //ha //Implicit return print(retNone() == [] ? "true" : "false"); //true //Global var access tab glob = "hi"; print(greet()); //hi //Self function call print(abc.decorate()); //eeeeee //Alternate self funcion call print(decorate(abc)); //eeeeee //Function overloading print(fibonacci(10).decorate()); //eeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee print(fibonacci(20, 3)); //text here. lorem ipsum //stdlib includes a function called 'upper' to turn tables into uppercase. However, we declared a function called the same. See what happens when declaring the import: print("j".upper()); //oops print("j".stdlib::upper()); //J print("j".local::upper()); //oops //Complex funcion usage + library functions print(join([getDate()[0, 3].reverse().join("/"), getDate()[3, 3].join(":")], " ")); //Todays date //Exiting the script compeltely(not needed at the end, used for early exit, maybe even in a function) exit; //Functions go AFTER all of the program function plusA(t){ return t + "a"; } function retNone(){ print("hello!"); //Implicitly returns [] } function greet(){ return glob; } function fibonacci(n){ if n <= 1{ return n; } //Recursion return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2); } function fibonacci(n, k){ return "text here. lorem ipsum"; } //Self function function decorate(self){ tab i = 0; tab r; while i < self{ r += "e"; i += 1; } return r; } //Local function function upper(self){ return "oops"; }

docs/introduction.html.title: Introduction | TableScript
docs/introduction.html.summary: Introduction Using the scripting language without any additional features is very straightforward: using TabScript; class Program{ static void Main(){ //Get source string src = File.ReadAllText("program.tbs"); try{ TableScript script = TableScript.FromSource("program.tbs", src); script.Run(); }catch(TabScriptException ex){ Console.Error.WriteLine(ex); } } } You can use a custom IImportResolver to add features in the form of imports. We will explore this feature further in a following tutorial. In the next tutorial, the central concept of a table will be explained.

docs/libraries.html.title: Libraries | TableScript
docs/libraries.html.summary: Libraries In this tutorial, we will create a library of C# code to import into TableScript. To create a custom library with external functions, start by creating a class with static methods: using TabScript; public static class CustomLib{ public static int sum1(int a){ return a + 1; } public static string getDayOfTheWeek(int index){ return index switch{ 1 => "Monday", 2 => "Tuesday", 3 => "Wednesday", 4 => "Thursday", 5 => "Friday", 6 => "Saturday", 7 => "Sunday", _ => null }; } } Because TableScript functions always return Table and take Table as arguments, they need to be converted. For the methods, the valid return types are: void: Returns an empty Table string: Returns a single element table. If the string is null, an empty table int: Returns a number table (new Table(int)) bool: Returns a 0 or 1 element table (Table.GetBool(bool)) Table: No conversion is done For the arguments, they must be one of the following: string: The table argument is reduced (Table.AsString()) int: The table argumet Length is used (Table.Length) bool: The table truthiness(length > 0) is used (Table.Truthy) Table: No conversion is done Now, add a property to convert to a ResolvedImport. Once this conversion is done, there is no point in doing it again, so we store it in a private field. Also, creating a list with functions and their description will make it easier. public static (Delegate func, string description)[] AllFunctions => new (Delegate, string)[]{ (sum1, "Sum 1 to a number"), (getDayOfTheWeek, "Get day of the week name by index") }; static ResolvedImport compiled = null; public static ResolvedImport AsImport {get{ if(compiled == null){ compiled = Library.BuildLibrary("customlib", AllFunctions); } return compiled; }} As we saw previously, we can use a custom IImportResolver. In this example, we will implement a Resolver on top of the Standard Resolver that can resolve this new library. This is the custom IImportResolver: using TabScript; class CustomImportResolver : StandardImportResolver{ public override ResolvedImport Resolve(string import, string callingFilename){ if(import == "customlib"){ return CustomLib.AsImport; } return base.Resolve(import, callingFilename); //Safely handle anything that wasnt recognized } } Then, we just need the Main method: using TabScript; class Program{ static void Main(){ string path = "program.tbs"; try{ TableScript script = TableScript.FromSource(path, File.ReadAllText(path), new CustomImportResolver()); script.Run(); }catch(TabScriptException ex){ Console.Error.WriteLine(ex); } } } Now, using this CustomImportResolver, we will have acess to sum1 and getDayOfTheWeek: // IMPORTANT // In this file, next to print() calls, the supposed output will be shown in a comment along with a commentary if needed import stdlib; import stdnum; import customlib; //Our custom library! print(sum1(3).toNum()); //4 print(getDayOfTheWeek(5)); //Friday If there is any doubt about the signature of functions implemented in libraries, you can always print them out: Console.WriteLine(CustomLib.AsImport); This prints: customlib export function sum1(a){ EXTERN; } //Sum 1 to a number export function getDayOfTheWeek(index){ EXTERN; } //Get day of the week name by index

docs/multifiles.html.title: Multiple Files | TableScript
docs/multifiles.html.summary: Multiple Files Combining multiple files helps you to organize your code. How can we make it possible? As we saw previously, we can use a custom IImportResolver. In this example, we will implement a Resolver on top of the Standard Resolver that can resolve files and merge them together. This is the custom IImportResolver: using TabScript; class CustomImportResolver : StandardImportResolver{ string path; //Keep the path public CustomImportResolver(string p){ path = p; } public override ResolvedImport Resolve(string import, string callingFilename){ if(File.Exists(path + "/" + import)){ return TableScript.SourceAsImport(path + "/" + import, File.ReadAllText(path + "/" + import), OnReport); } return base.Resolve(import, callingFilename); //Safely handle anything that wasnt recognized } } Then, we just need the Main method: using TabScript; class Program{ static void Main(){ string path = "program.tbs"; //Local path try{ TableScript script = TableScript.FromSource(path, File.ReadAllText(path), new CustomImportResolver(".")); script.Run(); }catch(TabScriptException ex){ Console.Error.WriteLine(ex); } } } When doing this, the functions marked export of the second file will be available for use. Here is an example of a two-file program, intentionally complicated to show what can and cannot be done. // IMPORTANT // In this file, next to print() calls, the supposed output will be shown in a comment along with a commentary if needed import stdlib; //Nothing happens for duplicated imports import "multifiles2.tbs"; //Import the other file. Cyclic imports is not something to worry about tab a = "Hello world"; //Global variable tab b = "Greetings!"; //Global variable print(greet()); //Greetings! print(greet2()); //I have always been ready to greet you print(greet3()); //I have always been ready to greet you print(greet4()); //I have always been ready to greet you print(greet5()); //I have always been ready to greet you print(greet6()); //Greetings! print(bye()); function fun(){ return b; //Accessing local variables } export function greet(){ return fun(); //Can access function that isnt export } import stdlib; //Nothing happens for duplicated imports import "multifiles1.tbs"; //Import the other file. Cyclic imports is not something to worry about import stdnum; //Only in this file tab a = "I have always been"; //Global variable tab b = " ready to greet you"; //Global variable print("Welcome!"); //This wont execute. Of the main body of imported scripts, only variable declarations are kept //Returns "I have always been", "ready to greet you" function fun(){ return a + b; //Accessing local variables } //same name as function in other file export function greet(){ return fun(); //Can access function that isnt export } export function greet2(){ return local::fun(); //Accesses local function } export function greet3(){ return greet(); //Accesses the local function, locals are prioritised if unspecified import } export function greet4(){ return local::greet(); //Accesses local function } export function greet5(){ return multifiles2_tbs::greet(); //Accesses local function with its import name } export function greet6(){ return multifiles1_tbs::greet(); //Accesses the function in the other file specifying the import } export function bye(){ //Stdnum is only available in this file if stdnum::greater(stdnum::rand("0", "1"), "0.5"){ print("Sad to see you go!"); }else{ print("Bye!"); } }

docs/operators.html.title: Operators | TableScript
docs/operators.html.summary: Operators In this tutorial, we will go over all operators available in TableScript. Concatenation operator + is the binary concatenation operator. It concatenates two tables together, appending the elements of the right table at the end of the elements of the left: tab greeting = "hello" + [" ", "world"]; print(greeting); //prints hello world You can also use its variation +=, that sets a table to itself concatenated to another: tab a = ["a", "b"]; a += "c"; print(a); //prints abc These operators work as expected(performing integer arithmetic) with number tables. += is also available for element-wise setting, reducing the table to a single string: tab a = ["a", "b"]; a.0 += ["h", "2"]; print(a == ["ah2", "b"]); //prints true Difference operator - is the binary difference operator. It removes elements of the right table contained in the left table(once per element): tab a = ["a", "a", "b"]; tab b = a - ["a"]; tab c = a - ["a", "a"]; print(b); //prints ab print(c); //prints b You can also use its variation -=, that sets a table to itself removing the elements of another: tab a = ["a", "a", "b"]; a -= ["a"]; print(a); //prints ab These operators work as expected(performing integer arithmetic) with number tables. Product operator * is the binary product operator. It performs a cartesian product between the elements of two tables: tab a = ["a", "b"] * ["c", "d"]; print(a); //prints acbcadbd tab b = ["a", "b"] * 3; print(b); //prints ababab tab c = 3 * ["a", "b"]; print(c); //prints aaabbb You can also use its variation *=, that sets a table to the cartesian product of itself with another: tab a = ["a", "b"]; a *= 3; print(a); //prints ababab These operators work as expected(performing integer arithmetic) with number tables. Equality operators == is the binary equality operator. != is the binary inequality operator. They test for equality element by element, and return a boolean table(0 or 1): print(["a", "b"] != ["ab"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints true print(["a", "b"] == ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints true print(["a", "b"] == "ab" ? "true" : "false"); //prints false Negation operator ! is the unary prefix negation operator. It returns false(0) if a table would be considered true(length > 0) and true(1) if a table would be considered false: tab t = !["h"]; //["h"] would be considered true because its length is greater than 0 print(t ? "true" : "false"); //prints false Ternary operator ? and : are the ternary conditional operator. It returns the first expression if the condition is true, and the second if its false: tab a = ["a", "b", "c"]; tab b = ["a", "b"]; print(a == b ? "true" : "false"); //prints false print(a == a ? "true" : "false"); //prints true Comparison operators >, >=, < and <= are the binary comparison operators. They return a boolean(true(1) or false(0)), returning true if the length of the first table is greater, greater or equal, less or less or equal than the length of the second table: print(["1"] > ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints false print(4 > ["a", "b"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints true Reduction operator ^ is the unary postfix reduction operator. It reduces a table to a single element table joining all its string elements: tab a = ["a", "b", "c", "def", "g"]; tab b = a^; print(b == ["abcdefg"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints true Decomposition operator % is the unary postfix decomposition operator. It splits all the string elements into chars: tab a = ["abc", "def", "g"]; tab b = a%; print(b == ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e", "f", "g"] ? "true" : "false"); //prints true Containment operator @ is the binary containment operator. It checks if all elements of the left table are contained on the right table, and returns a boolean(true(1) or false(0)): tab d = ["c", "d", "a", "n", "b"]; print(["a", "b"] @ d ? "true" : "false"); //prints true print(["a", "b", "m"] @ d ? "true" : "false"); //prints false It is also used in the syntax of foreach loops: tab d = ["c", "d", "a", "n", "b"]; foreach l @ d{ print(l); } In the next tutorial, an example program with virtually all features will be explained.

docs/stdlist.html.title: StdList tutorial | TableScript
docs/stdlist.html.summary: StdList tutorial Lists are several tables expressed as one, compacted together. Here is a program going over most of the features of stdlist: // IMPORTANT // In this file, next to print() calls, the supposed output will be shown in a comment along with a commentary if needed import stdlib; import stdnum; import stdlist; //StdList tutorial. Lists are several tables expressed as one, compacted together. tab lst1 = ["aa", "a", "aa", "t0e0", "t1e0", "t1e1"]; //Internal format used printLst(lst1); tab lst2 = newLst(); //New empty list lst2 = lst2.addTab(["h", "g"]); lst2 = lst2.addTab(["1", "2", "3", "4"]); lst2 = lst2.addTab([]); printLst(lst2); tab lst3 = mergeLst(lst1, lst2); printLst(lst3); tab lst4 = lst3; lst4 = lst4.delTab(4); lst4 = lst4.delTab(2); lst4 = lst4.setTab(2, "help"%); lst4 = lst4.setTabElem(2, 3, "7"); printLst(lst4); print(lst4.flattenLst().decor()); //Flattened all tables of the list tab lst5 = newLst(); lst5 = lst5.addTab(["b", "g"]); lst5 = lst5.addTab(["b", "h"]); lst5 = lst5.addTab(["b", "f"]); lst5 = lst5.insertTab(1, ["ñ", "k"]); printLst(lst5); print(tf(lst5.containsTab(["b", "h"]))); //true print(lst5.indexOfTab(["b", "h"]).toNum()); //Helper function function tf(self){ return self ? "true" : "false"; } //Helper function function decor(self){ return "[" + self.join(", ") + "]"; } //Function to print a list function printLst(self){ print(""); //Separator print(self.decor()); tab isLst = self.isLst(); print("Is list: " + tf(isLst)); if(!isLst){ return; //Exit if its not a list } tab len = self.lstLen(); print("List length: " + len.toNum()); tab i = 0; while(i < len){ //Printing all tables tab tablen = self.tabLen(i); print("Table " + i.toNum() + " length: " + tablen.toNum()); print("Table " + i.toNum() + ": " + self.getTab(i).decor()); tab j = 0; while(j < tablen){ //Printing all elements inside table print("Table " + i.toNum() + " element " + j.toNum() + ": " + self.getTabElem(i, j)); j += 1; } i += 1; } }

docs/tables.html.title: Understanding Tables | TableScript
docs/tables.html.summary: Understanding Tables Tables are the only variable type in TableScript. They are a collection of strings, but can also represent integers, booleans and strings. Internally, they are represented as a string collection or as a number(used to save resources on integer tables). Tables as string collections Elements of the table can be accessed in TableScript in two ways: Using the dot syntax Using the square brackets syntax //Table initialization tab a = ["a", "b", "c"]; //Dot syntax print(a.0); //prints a //Square brackets syntax print(a[1]); //prints b //Out of bounds index returns [] print(a[100]); //Negative indices print(a[-1]); //prints c Negative indices start by the end, -1 being the last element, -2 the previous one and so forth. The square syntax allows to use full expressions as indices, something the dot syntax doesnt. You can also access a random element using the random keyword. Tables as integers A table's length is its value as an integer. Also, you can access a tables length using the length keyword. This keyword returns a number table (table made up of empty strings): //Table initialization tab a = ["a", "b", "c"]; print(a.length == ["", "", ""] ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true This is also what happens when using numbers. For example 0 represents a table of length 0([]) and 3 represents a table of length 3(["", "", ""]): //Table initialization tab a = 3; print(a == ["", "", ""] ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true Numbers allow you to set negative lengths for tables, something that doesnt make much sense, and is turned positive as soon as they stop having empty elements: //Table initialization tab a = -4; a.0 = "h"; //Setting an element print(a.length == 1 ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true Thats why it is recommended not using negative number tables for other things that arent numbers. Tables as booleans Tables are defined as being true when their length is greater than 0. Many operators that return booleans (such as ==) return either 0 or 1 for false and true: tab a = ["a", "b"] == ["a", "b", "c"]; print(a == 0 ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true Also, for conditions in statements, any table can be used: tab a = ["a", "b"]; if a{ print("a is greater than 0"); } Tables as strings Tables are collections of strings, so to use them as strings, all elements are flattened into a length 1 table. This is the same that happens with the reduction operator(postfix ^): tab a = ["a", "b", "c"]; print(a^ == "abc" ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true Also, initializing tables as a single string creates them with length 1: print("abc" == ["abc"] ? "true" : "false"); //Prints true In the next tutorial, we will go over all the operators available in TableScript.

index.html.title: TableScript | TableScript
index.html.summary: TableScript TableScript is a string-based scripting languange. This library built in C# provides an API to interpret TableScript programs. You can go to the Introduction for starting. You can also check the API for the code usage details.
